name: Phase 8 · File & Blob Storage
short: Local disk → object storage → CDN → multi-region edge — because binary data has no business living in your database

detail.what: File & blob storage is the infrastructure for unstructured binary data: user uploads (profile photos, documents, videos), system-generated files (PDF reports, CSV exports, resized image variants), backups, and logs. Unlike structured data (rows in a database), binary blobs are opaque byte sequences that are written once, read many times, and rarely updated in place. Object storage (AWS S3, Google Cloud Storage, Cloudflare R2) is the canonical answer for this workload — it provides a flat key-value namespace (think of it as a giant hash map: key = 'users/42/avatar.jpg', value = the bytes of that image), virtually infinite capacity (S3 stores exabytes of data across all customers), HTTP-native access (every object gets a URL), and durability to 11 nines (99.999999999% — S3 is designed to lose less than 1 object per 10 billion objects stored per year, achieved by automatically replicating each object across at least 3 physically separated facilities). Object storage is not a file system — there are no directories, no file locks, no append operations. It's optimized for 'write whole object, read whole object, delete whole object.' If you need to update a file, you upload a new version. This simplicity is what makes it virtually infinitely scalable. Adding a CDN (Content Delivery Network) in front transforms your storage from 'files in one datacenter' to 'files cached at 300+ locations worldwide.' The CDN keeps copies of popular files at edge PoPs (Points of Presence) near users, so the 95% of requests for popular content are served in <10ms from the nearest PoP, while only the 5% long-tail requests actually reach your origin storage. Think of the CDN as a global cache layer specifically for static files.
detail.why: Binary data in your database is one of the most expensive mistakes in system design, and it's disturbingly common in early-stage applications because it's the easiest path ('just add a BYTEA column!'). Here's what actually happens: a 5MB image stored as a PostgreSQL BLOB bloats your table by 5MB per row. With 100K users, that's 500GB of images mixed in with your transactional data. Your nightly backup that used to take 5 minutes now takes 2 hours — and it's backing up the same images every night even though they never change. Your database cache (shared_buffers in Postgres) is now full of image bytes instead of hot query data, so your actual transactional queries are slower. Your replication lag increases because every image upload ships 5MB across the wire to each replica. Your pg_dump takes forever, your PITR (point-in-time recovery) archives balloon, and your cloud storage bill is $0.10–0.20/GB/month instead of S3's $0.023/GB/month — a 5–10× cost premium for worse performance. Object storage solves all of these problems: it's purpose-built for binary data, costs 5–10× less, scales infinitely without affecting your transactional database, and integrates natively with CDNs for global delivery. The pattern is simple and universal: store the file in S3, store the URL/key in your database, serve via CDN. Every major platform — Instagram, Twitter, Dropbox, Slack, Figma — uses this pattern. The only question is how sophisticated your CDN and transformation pipeline needs to be.
detail.numbers: Cost comparison per GB/month: S3 Standard = $0.023, S3 Infrequent Access = $0.0125, S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval = $0.004, S3 Glacier Deep Archive = $0.00099, Google Cloud Storage Standard = $0.020, Cloudflare R2 = $0.015 (with zero egress fees — this is the key differentiator), Backblaze B2 = $0.006. For comparison: RDS/Postgres storage = $0.10–0.20/GB/month, which is 5–10× more expensive and worse at serving files. CDN cache hit ratio target: >90% for static assets (images, JS, CSS); at >95% you've essentially eliminated origin load for popular content. S3 durability: 11 nines (99.999999999%) — designed to sustain the concurrent loss of data in two facilities without data loss. S3 availability: 99.9% for Standard (43 min/month downtime possible), 99.99% with cross-region replication. Pre-signed URL flow: client requests upload URL from your API (~5ms), your API generates a pre-signed S3 PUT URL with 15-minute expiry (~1ms, no S3 call needed — it's a local cryptographic operation), client uploads directly to S3 (bypasses your servers entirely), S3 confirms upload, client notifies your API of the completed upload. This eliminates the double-bandwidth problem and keeps your app servers out of the data path. Bandwidth costs: S3 egress = $0.09/GB (first 10TB/month), CloudFront egress = $0.085/GB (cheaper than S3 direct!), Cloudflare = $0 egress (R2 and CDN), Backblaze B2 + Cloudflare = $0 egress (Bandwidth Alliance). At scale, egress costs dwarf storage costs — a petabyte of egress on S3 costs $90K/month.

tradeoffs.left: Origin-only: cheapest, but 50–300ms latency for distant users depending on geography
tradeoffs.right: CDN: adds ~$0.01–0.085/GB egress cost, but <10ms latency from any major city on earth

tradeoffs.left: Multi-region replication: 11+ nines, survives regional disaster, but 2× storage cost
tradeoffs.right: Single region: 11 nines durability (still extraordinary), cheaper, but a full-region loss means restoration from backup

tradeoffs.left: Serve original files only: zero processing pipeline, simple ops, but large payloads and no device optimization
tradeoffs.right: On-the-fly transformation (resize, format-convert, compress): optimal payloads per device, but adds processing cost and cache complexity

pitfalls.name: Proxying file uploads through your app
pitfalls.desc: User uploads a 100MB video → your app server receives all 100MB into memory → then forwards those same 100MB to S3. You've paid for the bandwidth twice (user→server, server→S3), consumed 100MB of your app server's RAM for the duration (blocking other requests if you're not streaming), and added latency (the user waits for both hops sequentially). The solution is pre-signed URLs: your API generates a time-limited, cryptographically signed URL that grants the client permission to PUT directly to S3. The client uploads straight to S3 — your server never touches the bytes. After upload completes, the client calls your API to register the file's S3 key. This is the standard pattern used by Slack (file uploads), Figma (design files), and every major SaaS. In AWS, use createPresignedPost() from the S3 SDK; in GCP, use generateSignedUrl(). Set expiry to 15–60 minutes and restrict the key prefix so users can only upload to their own namespace.

pitfalls.name: Public S3 bucket leaks
pitfalls.desc: This is the single most common cloud security incident: an S3 bucket accidentally configured as public, exposing customer data, internal documents, or database backups to the entire internet. Capital One (2019, 100M customer records), Twitch (2021, full source code leak), and dozens of smaller companies have been burned. The fix is defense-in-depth: (1) Enable AWS S3 Block Public Access at the account level — this is a master switch that prevents ANY bucket from being made public, regardless of individual bucket policies; (2) Default every bucket to private; (3) Use CloudFront or Cloudflare as the public-facing layer with signed URLs or signed cookies for access control — users never interact with S3 directly; (4) Use AWS Config rules or open-source tools like Prowler to continuously audit bucket policies and alert on any public access. For sensitive data (PII, health records), add S3 Object Lock (WORM — Write Once Read Many) to prevent even administrators from deleting compliance-required data.

pitfalls.name: No lifecycle policy
pitfalls.desc: User uploads from 2018 are still sitting in S3 Standard at full price ($0.023/GB/month). Log files from 3 years ago that nobody will ever read again are costing you thousands per month. S3 Lifecycle Policies automate tier transitions: move objects to Infrequent Access after 90 days (45% cheaper), to Glacier Instant Retrieval after 1 year (83% cheaper, millisecond retrieval), to Glacier Deep Archive after 3 years (96% cheaper, 12-hour retrieval), and delete after 7 years (or whatever your compliance requires). For dynamic workloads where you can't predict access patterns, S3 Intelligent Tiering automatically moves objects between tiers based on actual access — it costs an extra $0.0025/1K objects/month for monitoring but eliminates the need to guess. At 100TB of stored data, proper lifecycle policies save $15K–25K/year.

pitfalls.name: Forgetting CDN cache invalidation
pitfalls.desc: You update a user's avatar — they upload a new photo to the same path 'avatars/user-42.jpg'. S3 now has the new version, but CloudFront cached the old version at 300+ PoPs and will keep serving it for hours (default TTL). The user refreshes and sees their old face. Two solutions: (1) Content-addressed URLs (the right way): include a hash of the file content in the URL — 'avatars/user-42-a8f3bc9d.jpg'. When the avatar changes, the URL changes, which is a guaranteed cache miss → CDN fetches the new version immediately. Old URLs still work (old cached version is fine since nobody references it anymore). This is how every modern frontend build tool works (webpack content hashes). (2) CDN Invalidation API (the escape hatch): call CloudFront's CreateInvalidation or Cloudflare's Purge Cache API to explicitly remove the old version from all PoPs. This takes 5–30 seconds to propagate globally and costs $0.005 per path on CloudFront (first 1,000/month free). Use this sparingly — it's slow and doesn't scale. Content-addressed URLs are always preferred.

pitfalls.name: Client-side CDN URL hardcoded
pitfalls.desc: You ship a mobile app that references images at 'https://d1234abcdef8.cloudfront.net/images/logo.png'. Six months later you want to switch from CloudFront to Cloudflare (cheaper, zero egress) or add a multi-CDN failover layer. But that CloudFront URL is baked into the binary of every mobile app in the wild — and 30% of your users are on app versions more than 6 months old. You literally cannot change the CDN without abandoning those users. The fix: always use your own domain as a CNAME — 'cdn.yourapp.com' — which you point at your CDN via DNS. Switching CDNs becomes a DNS change (seconds, no app update). This also lets you implement multi-CDN (Cloudflare primary, CloudFront fallback) by routing at the DNS level.

pitfalls.name: Not optimizing images for device
pitfalls.desc: You serve the original 4000×3000 JPEG (3.5MB) from a user's DSLR camera to every device — including a mobile phone that displays it at 375px wide. The user downloads 3.5MB when a 375px WebP at quality 80 would be 35KB — a 100× difference. On a 3G connection, that's the difference between 200ms and 20 seconds. Use an image transformation pipeline: either on-upload (generate preset sizes: thumbnail, medium, large) using a Lambda/Cloud Function triggered by the S3 upload event, or on-the-fly using a CDN-integrated image service (Cloudflare Image Resizing, Imgix, Cloudinary) that transforms on first request and caches the result. On-the-fly is more flexible (any size, any format, responsive to client hints) but adds first-request latency; pre-generation is faster to serve but requires predicting every size you'll need.

pitfalls.name: No virus/malware scanning on uploads
pitfalls.desc: Users upload files, you store them, other users download them. Without scanning, you've become a malware distribution platform. Add an asynchronous scan step: S3 event notification triggers a Lambda that runs ClamAV (open source, free) or a commercial scanner (Trend Micro, CrowdStrike) on every uploaded file. Quarantine files that fail the scan. This is especially critical for file-sharing, healthcare, and financial applications where regulatory requirements mandate it.

examples.name: Instagram photo storage at scale — from S3 to Haystack
examples.desc: Instagram started on S3, which is the correct choice for nearly everyone. But at billions of photos with each photo needing 4+ size variants (thumbnail, small, medium, large) for different device sizes, S3's per-object metadata overhead became significant. Each S3 object carries ~1KB of metadata — at 100 billion objects, that's 100TB of metadata alone, which affects listing performance and costs. Facebook (Instagram's parent) built Haystack, a purpose-built photo store that packs many photos into a single large file (like a TAR archive) with a custom index. This reduced per-photo metadata from ~1KB to ~10 bytes — a 100× improvement. The lesson isn't 'don't use S3' — it's 'S3 is correct until you have tens of billions of objects, at which point you have the engineering team and budget to build something custom.' Instagram used S3 successfully for years before needing Haystack.

examples.name: Imgur on a CDN-heavy architecture
examples.desc: Imgur, one of the internet's largest image hosts, serves 99%+ of traffic from CDN. Their origin storage (S3) is essentially write-only — it receives new uploads and serves the rare cache miss, but the CDN handles virtually all reads. Without aggressive CDN caching, their bandwidth bill would be 100× higher (potentially millions per month). The architecture is beautifully simple: upload → S3 → CDN → users. The CDN TTL is set to 1 year for images (they never change), and the URL includes a content hash so new versions get new URLs. This 'immutable objects + long TTL + content-addressed URLs' pattern is the gold standard for image-heavy applications.

examples.name: Dropbox's hybrid storage evolution
examples.desc: Dropbox started on AWS S3 and ran there for years. As they grew to exabytes of data, the cost of S3 storage + egress became enormous — hundreds of millions per year. In 2015–2017, they executed 'Magic Pocket': migrating 90% of their data to custom-built storage running on their own hardware in leased datacenters. They reported saving hundreds of millions of dollars. The remaining 10% (the long tail and burst capacity) stayed on S3. This demonstrates the cloud-to-colo crossover point: at extreme scale (petabytes+), owning hardware is 2–5× cheaper than cloud, but only if you have the engineering team to build and operate it (Dropbox invested hundreds of engineers over years). For 99.9% of companies, S3 is the right answer indefinitely.

examples.name: Cloudflare R2's zero-egress disruption
examples.desc: In 2022, Cloudflare launched R2 — an S3-compatible object store with zero egress fees. This was a direct attack on AWS's most profitable hidden cost. S3 egress charges $0.09/GB — at scale, companies were paying more for getting data OUT of S3 than for storing it. R2 eliminated this entirely: store at $0.015/GB/month, retrieve for free. For bandwidth-heavy workloads (image hosting, video delivery, CDN origin), R2 saves 50–80% vs S3+CloudFront. The trade-off: R2 is single-region (no cross-region replication yet), has fewer features than S3 (no lifecycle policies yet, limited event notifications), and the ecosystem is less mature. But for 'store files, serve via CDN,' it's arguably the best value in cloud storage today.

levelUp.trigger: Any user-uploaded file or file >1MB, or your database backup exceeds 10GB because of stored BLOBs
levelUp.action: Migrate all binary data to S3/GCS/R2 immediately — this is non-negotiable. Here's the step-by-step: (1) Create an S3 bucket (private, Block Public Access enabled, versioning on, default encryption AES-256). (2) For each binary column in your DB, write a migration script that reads the blob, uploads to S3 at a deterministic key (e.g., 'uploads/{table}/{id}/{filename}'), stores the S3 key in a new VARCHAR column, and NULLs out the old blob column after verification. Run this as an idempotent backfill job (safe to rerun). (3) Update your application code: writes go to S3 first (using pre-signed URLs for client uploads), then store the key in the DB. Reads construct an S3 or CDN URL from the stored key. (4) After verifying the migration, drop the blob columns and reclaim space (VACUUM FULL in Postgres). (5) Set up a basic lifecycle policy: move to Infrequent Access after 90 days. This migration typically takes 1–2 weeks of engineering effort and immediately reduces your DB size by 50–90%, making backups faster, replication cheaper, and queries snappier.

levelUp.trigger: >100GB stored in object storage, or users are globally distributed and experiencing >500ms file load times, or image bandwidth exceeds $500/month
levelUp.action: Put a CDN in front of your object storage and build an image optimization pipeline. Implementation: (1) CDN setup: create a CloudFront distribution (or Cloudflare zone) with your S3 bucket as origin. Set default TTL to 24h for mutable content, 1 year for content-addressed URLs. Enable gzip/brotli compression for text-based files. Cost: CloudFront free tier includes 1TB/month transfer. (2) Pre-signed URLs for uploads: implement the client-direct-upload pattern. Your API generates a pre-signed S3 PUT URL (15-min expiry, scoped to the user's key prefix). Client uploads directly, then notifies your API. This eliminates your servers from the upload data path. (3) Image optimization pipeline: set up a Lambda function triggered by S3 PutObject events that generates 3–4 size variants (thumbnail 150px, small 400px, medium 800px, large 1600px) in WebP format with quality 80. Store variants alongside the original. Alternatively, use Imgix ($3/1000 master images/month) or Cloudinary (free tier: 25K transformations/month) for on-the-fly transformations — more flexible, less ops, but more expensive at scale. (4) Serve images via CDN with content-addressed URLs: 'cdn.yourapp.com/images/{hash}-{width}.webp'. (5) Enable S3 Intelligent Tiering to automatically move old files to cheaper tiers.

levelUp.trigger: CDN cache miss rate >10% (indicating long-tail content that doesn't fit the standard caching model), or edge latency SLO <30ms globally, or storage exceeds 1PB, or bandwidth costs are a major line item
levelUp.action: Deploy a multi-CDN strategy with edge-native storage and smart routing. Implementation: (1) Multi-CDN: use Cloudflare as primary (best global coverage, cheapest egress) and CloudFront as fallback (best for AWS-origin traffic, most configurable). A CDN load balancer like Cedexis/Citrix ITM or NS1 routes requests to the CDN with the best current performance for that user's geography. This also provides CDN-level redundancy — if Cloudflare has an outage (rare but it happens), traffic automatically shifts to CloudFront. (2) Edge storage: replicate frequently-accessed content to R2 or S3 in multiple regions, with the CDN configured to pull from the nearest origin. For truly global sub-30ms serving, consider Cloudflare Workers KV or Durable Objects for metadata and routing, with R2 for the actual blobs. (3) Smart routing and content-aware optimization: Cloudflare Image Resizing or Fastly Image Optimizer (runs Wasm at the edge) transforms images on-the-fly at the PoP closest to the user — no origin round-trip for size variants. (4) Video: implement adaptive bitrate streaming (HLS or DASH) using AWS MediaConvert or FFmpeg pipelines to transcode uploads into multiple bitrate ladder rungs. Serve via CDN with byte-range requests. (5) Cost optimization: audit your storage tiers — at 1PB, even a $0.005/GB/month savings (e.g., moving from S3 Standard to Glacier Instant for cold data) saves $5K/month. Consider Cloudflare R2 for origin storage if egress is a major cost driver.

small.range: <10GB total, few file types (avatars, document uploads)
small.rec: Use S3 (or GCS/R2) from day one, even for your MVP — never store files in PostgreSQL, MySQL, or any transactional database. The migration out is painful and the performance degradation sneaks up on you. Use pre-signed URLs for client uploads from the start — it's only ~20 lines of code and saves you from ever proxying file bytes through your app server. Set bucket policy to private with Block Public Access enabled. Store the S3 key in your database, construct the public URL at read time (either a pre-signed GET URL for private files with 1-hour expiry, or a CDN URL for public files). Technology comparison: AWS S3 ($0.023/GB/month, the gold standard, most features, largest ecosystem, best documentation) vs Google Cloud Storage ($0.020/GB/month, slightly cheaper, excellent integration with GCP ML services) vs Cloudflare R2 ($0.015/GB/month, zero egress fees — this is huge if you serve lots of downloads, S3-compatible API, but fewer features and single-region) vs Backblaze B2 ($0.006/GB/month, cheapest storage, S3-compatible API, pairs with Cloudflare for zero egress via Bandwidth Alliance, but smaller feature set and less battle-tested at extreme scale). For most startups: S3 if you're on AWS, GCS if you're on GCP, R2 if bandwidth costs matter more than advanced features.

medium.range: 10GB–10TB, multiple file types, users expect fast loads
medium.rec: S3 + CloudFront CDN for serving static assets globally. This is the 'standard architecture' that serves 90% of companies from seed to Series C. For image-heavy apps, add an image transformation pipeline: either Lambda-on-upload (simplest: S3 event triggers Lambda that runs Sharp/libvips to generate size variants — ~$0.20 per 1M invocations) or a managed service like Imgix ($3/1K master images/month, on-the-fly transforms via URL parameters — 'img.imgix.net/photo.jpg?w=400&fm=webp') or Cloudinary (free tier: 25K transformations/month, then ~$89/month for Plus). Imgix is ideal for read-heavy image serving (fast, CDN-integrated, no compute cost per transform after cache); Cloudinary excels for upload-time transformations, video, and AI-powered cropping. Set S3 lifecycle: 90 days → Infrequent Access (saves 45%), 1 year → Glacier Instant Retrieval (saves 83%). Use S3 Intelligent Tiering ($0.0025/1K objects/month monitoring fee) if access patterns are unpredictable. Signed URLs for download should expire in 1 hour for general files, 15 minutes for sensitive documents. Enable S3 versioning for user uploads — it's cheap insurance against accidental overwrites and enables 'undo' features.

large.range: 10TB–1PB, video content, global users, complex media pipeline
large.rec: Multi-CDN strategy for resilience and cost optimization: Cloudflare as primary (300+ PoPs, cheapest egress, integrated image resizing at $0.00/resize for first 100K/month on Pro plan), CloudFront as secondary (tightest AWS integration, 400+ PoPs, $0.085/GB). Use a CDN performance monitor (Cedexis, Catchpoint) to route each user to the fastest CDN in real time. Image optimization at CDN edge: Cloudflare Polish (lossless/lossy optimization, free on Pro plan), Fastly Image Optimizer (Wasm-powered at edge, extremely fast, ~$0.02/transform), or Imgix (URL-based, supports all formats including AVIF and WebP). For video: transcode uploads to adaptive bitrate HLS using AWS MediaConvert ($0.024/min for HD output) or FFmpeg on ECS/Lambda. Generate a bitrate ladder (360p, 720p, 1080p, 4K) so players automatically select the right quality for the user's bandwidth. Store HLS segments in S3, serve via CDN with long TTLs. Enable S3 Cross-Region Replication (CRR) to a secondary region for disaster recovery — at this scale, losing a region's worth of data is a company-ending event. Cost optimization: Cloudflare R2 for origin storage if egress is a significant cost (likely at this scale). Backblaze B2 + Cloudflare (Bandwidth Alliance = zero egress) for archival storage at $0.006/GB/month.

hyper.range: >1PB, billions of objects, global real-time serving, custom media pipeline
hyper.rec: At this scale, you're likely operating a custom storage tier alongside cloud storage. Distributed object storage across all regions with content-aware routing: hot content (accessed in last 24h, typically 5–10% of objects) lives on fast NVMe-backed storage at every major PoP; warm content (accessed in last 90 days) lives in S3/R2 in 2–3 regions; cold content lives in Glacier/tape. Smart routing at the CDN edge determines which tier to fetch from. Consider the Facebook approach: pack small objects (images, thumbnails) into large aggregate files (Haystack-style) to reduce per-object metadata overhead — at 100B+ objects, S3's per-object overhead is measurable. Custom storage formats for efficiency: store images in a columnar format optimized for range reads (fetch just the header for dimensions without reading the whole image). For video at exabyte scale: build or license a custom transcoding pipeline (FFmpeg on bare metal for cost efficiency at this volume — cloud transcoding at $0.024/min becomes millions per year). Content-aware CDN routing: use ML models to predict which content will go viral (based on engagement signals) and pre-warm CDN caches before the traffic spike arrives. Technologies: custom distributed storage (inspired by Facebook Haystack/f4, Google Colossus), erasure coding for storage efficiency (store data in N+K fragments across nodes — any N fragments can reconstruct the data, surviving K node failures with less overhead than full 3× replication), petabyte-scale management with automated tiering.

formulas.name: Monthly storage cost
formulas.note: S3 Standard includes 3× replication in the price ($0.023/GB). Cross-region replication doubles it. Example: 10TB in S3 Standard = 10,000 × $0.023 = $230/month. With CRR to a second region: $460/month.

formulas.name: Monthly egress/bandwidth cost
formulas.note: S3 direct egress: $0.09/GB. CloudFront egress: $0.085/GB (cheaper than S3!). Cloudflare/R2: $0/GB. Example: 50TB/month of image downloads. Via S3 direct: $4,500. Via CloudFront: $4,250. Via Cloudflare: $0. At scale, egress is the dominant cost — choose your CDN/storage accordingly.

formulas.name: CDN cache hit ratio
formulas.note: Target: >90% for static assets, >95% for immutable content-addressed URLs. Below 80% your CDN is barely earning its cost. Improve with longer TTLs, content-addressed URLs (cache forever), and cache warming for predictably popular content.

formulas.name: Image bandwidth savings
formulas.note: Converting JPEG to WebP at quality 80 typically saves 25–35%. Resizing a 4000px image to 800px saves ~95% of bytes. Combined (resize + format + compression): 90–99% savings per image. At 1M image serves/day, this can mean terabytes of bandwidth saved monthly.

formulas.name: Pre-signed URL upload flow time
formulas.note: api_latency (generate URL) ≈ 5ms. s3_upload_time = file_size / user_upload_bandwidth. callback_latency (register file) ≈ 10ms. For a 5MB file on a 10Mbps upload: 5ms + 4s + 10ms ≈ 4s total. Without pre-signed URL (proxied through server): 5ms + 4s (user→server) + 2s (server→S3) + 10ms ≈ 6s — 50% slower and your server RAM is occupied for the full 6 seconds.

antiPatterns.name: The 'file system in S3' antipattern
antiPatterns.desc: Treating S3 like a POSIX file system: listing directories, renaming files (S3 rename = copy + delete), relying on directory listings for application logic. S3's LIST operation is paginated (1,000 objects per call), eventually consistent for new listings, and charges $0.005/1,000 requests. If your application logic requires 'list all files in a folder and process each one,' use a database index (DynamoDB or Postgres table listing file keys) instead of S3 LIST. S3 is a key-value store, not a file system.

antiPatterns.name: The 'single key prefix' hotspot
antiPatterns.desc: All your files are keyed under 'uploads/2024/06/28/{filename}'. S3 partitions by key prefix — if all your writes share a date-based prefix, they hammer the same partition. While S3 has largely eliminated this problem with automatic partition splitting (since 2018), extremely high write rates (>5,500 PUT/s to the same prefix) can still hit limits. Use a hash prefix for write-heavy workloads: 'uploads/{hash(user_id)}/{date}/{filename}' distributes writes across partitions.

antiPatterns.name: The 'CDN for everything' antipattern
antiPatterns.desc: Caching user-specific private files on a shared CDN without signed URLs. Now user A's medical record is cached at a PoP, and if user B guesses the URL, the CDN serves it. Private files must use signed URLs (CloudFront signed URLs, Cloudflare signed tokens) with per-user, time-limited access. Alternatively, bypass the CDN entirely for private files and serve via pre-signed S3 GET URLs with 1-hour expiry.

decisionMatrix.scenario: User avatars and profile photos (<5MB each)
decisionMatrix.recommendation: S3 + CDN with content-addressed URLs. Pre-generate 3 sizes on upload (thumbnail 150px, medium 400px, large 800px) in WebP format. Cache forever (immutable URLs). This covers 80% of avatar use cases at minimal cost.

S3 with pre-signed upload URLs. Serve via pre-signed GET URLs (1-hour expiry) — don't cache private documents on CDN unless you implement signed URL validation at CDN level. Scan for malware on upload using ClamAV Lambda.
S3 for raw uploads. Transcode to HLS adaptive bitrate (AWS MediaConvert or FFmpeg). Store HLS segments in S3, serve via CDN with long TTLs. For live streaming: AWS IVS, Mux, or Cloudflare Stream.
S3 + CloudFront or Cloudflare Pages. Content-addressed filenames (app.a3f8bc9d.js). Cache-Control: max-age=31536000, immutable. This is the simplest and most effective use of CDN.

decisionMatrix.scenario: Large dataset distribution (>1GB files, open data)
decisionMatrix.recommendation: Cloudflare R2 or Backblaze B2 + Cloudflare (zero egress). For academic/open-source: consider AWS Open Data Sponsorship (free hosting for qualifying datasets).

from_database_blobs.description: Step-by-step migration from database BLOB/BYTEA columns to object storage — the most common storage migration
steps: 1. Create destination S3 bucket with: versioning enabled, Block Public Access on, default AES-256 encryption, lifecycle policy (90d → IA, 1y → Glacier).
2. Add a new column to the table (e.g., 'file_s3_key VARCHAR') alongside the existing BLOB column. Deploy code that reads from the new column if populated, falling back to the BLOB column.

3. Write an idempotent backfill script: for each row with a BLOB and no S3 key, upload the blob to S3 at a deterministic key (e.g., 'table_name/row_id/original_filename'), then UPDATE the row to set file_s3_key. Process in batches of 100–1000 with rate limiting to avoid overloading the DB.
4. Monitor: verify all rows have been migrated (SELECT COUNT(*) WHERE file_s3_key IS NULL AND blob_column IS NOT NULL = 0).
5. Deploy code that writes new files to S3 only (no more BLOB writes). Reads serve from S3 exclusively.
6. After a soak period (1–2 weeks), drop the BLOB column. Run VACUUM FULL (Postgres) or OPTIMIZE TABLE (MySQL) to reclaim disk space. Your database just shrank by 50–90%.
7. Verify backups are smaller and faster. Celebrate.
